content.assessment: A comprehensive de-tracking pilot combined with choice/zoning controls is the proposal most directly structured to test causal mechanisms rather than assume them, addressing both the early-tracking effect and the segregation channel operating through school choice. Evidence cautions that de-tracking alone is insufficient against choice-driven segregation absent institutional guardrails, which this proposal explicitly anticipates by linking to zoning reform (T4). Pilot non-representativeness and reversal risk (per Polish precedent) are legitimate threats to generalizability and durability of any equity gains found.
opportunities: Could generate Hungary-specific causal evidence resolving the contested question of whether track value-added is institutional or background-driven
Successful pairing with zoning controls could produce a replicable model addressing both tracking and segregation channels simultaneously
strengths: Explicitly pairs de-tracking with choice/zoning controls, addressing both mechanisms (tracking and geographic/choice-driven segregation) evidence treats as related but distinct
Designed to isolate Hungary-specific effects from confounding reforms, improving causal interpretability of results
Cross-national evidence found no significant efficiency losses from de-tracking, easing concern about achievement trade-offs
threats: Evidence that school-choice deregulation reintroduces segregation even without formal tracking means an under-resourced pilot risks failing to demonstrate feasible equity gains
Institutional durability precedent (Polish reversal) suggests even positive pilot results may not survive political transition to national policy
Volunteer pilot regions may be unrepresentative, limiting confidence in extrapolated distributional effects
weaknesses: Piloting delays national-scale equity benefit, leaving the currently documented severe SES-linked achievement gap and Roma dropout disparities unaddressed in non-pilot regions during the trial period
